title: Coordination-Resolved Surrogate Models for Thermodynamic Stability, Band Gaps, and Magnetic Moments of Spinel Oxides, Sulfides, and Selenides
summary: We curated 320 cubic ($Fd\bar{3}m$) spinel entries from the Materials Project-nitrides, oxides, sulfides, and selenides, including single-cation mixed-valence $A_3X_4$ compounds-and trained tree-ensemble surrogates for the formation energy, energy above the convex hull, band gap, total magnetization, and metallicity. Cations were assigned to tetrahedral-like and octahedral-like groups from CrystalNN coordination numbers rather than from element identity, and the evaluation was group-aware throughout: splits were grouped by reduced formula, every transform was fit on training folds only, and champion models were selected on cross-validated scores before the holdout was examined. Over twenty repeated grouped holdouts (single-seed refits that reuse the tuned hyperparameters, and are therefore mildly optimistic) the champions reach mean absolute errors of $0.121\pm0.030$ eV/atom for the formation energy, $0.048\pm0.013$ eV/atom for the hull distance, and $1.27\pm0.19$~\muBfu{} for the magnetization, with a metallicity accuracy of $0.85\pm0.06$. Band-gap regression does not beat a trivial baseline on the 19-member non-metal holdout under paired bootstrap testing, we report this negative result and trace it to sample scarcity and to the semi-local DFT labels. On the identical grouped split, the tabular champion is more accurate than an untuned MEGNet trained from scratch (0.087 versus 0.209 eV/atom formation-energy MAE on the primary holdout), a comparison that bounds, rather than settles, the descriptor-versus-graph question at this data scale. SHAP attribution ties the magnetization model to octahedral $d$-occupancy and the formation-energy and band-gap models to electronegativity descriptors, and grouped conformal intervals, permutation nulls, and leave-one-chemistry-out tests bound a domain of applicability that is uneven across anions and cations.

AI.tldr: This paper trains tree-ensemble machine learning models to predict formation energy, convex hull distance, band gap, magnetization, and metallicity for 320 cubic spinel compounds (nitrides, oxides, sulfides, selenides). Using CrystalNN coordination-based cation grouping rather than element identity, the models achieve 0.121 eV/atom MAE for formation energy, 0.048 eV/atom for hull distance, and 1.27 μB for magnetization, outperforming MEGNet on identical splits. However, band-gap prediction fails to beat trivial baselines due to sample scarcity and DFT limitations.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop accurate machine learning surrogates for predicting materials properties of spinel compounds, which are technologically important but computationally expensive to study via DFT. The authors address a key challenge in materials ML: ensuring proper data splitting and model evaluation that respects chemical similarity to prevent data leakage and provide realistic performance estimates.
AI.method: The method involves: (1) Curating 320 cubic spinel entries from Materials Project; (2) Assigning cations to tetrahedral/octahedral groups using CrystalNN coordination numbers rather than element identity; (3) Using group-aware evaluation with splits grouped by reduced formula; (4) Training tree-ensemble models (surrogates) for five target properties; (5) Comparing with MEGNet graph neural network; (6) Using SHAP for feature attribution; (7) Applying grouped conformal intervals, permutation tests, and leave-one-chemistry-out validation to bound domain of applicability.
AI.result: Over 20 repeated grouped holdouts: formation energy MAE = 0.121±0.030 eV/atom, hull distance MAE = 0.048±0.013 eV/atom, magnetization MAE = 1.27±0.19 μB, metallicity accuracy = 0.85±0.06. Band-gap regression fails to beat trivial baseline on 19-member non-metal holdout (negative result). Tabular champion outperforms untuned MEGNet (0.087 vs 0.209 eV/atom formation-energy MAE). SHAP shows magnetization tied to octahedral d-occupancy; formation energy and band gap linked to electronegativity descriptors.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that properly grouped cross-validation is crucial for realistic ML model评估 in materials science. Tree-ensemble models can outperform graph neural networks at this data scale when properly validated. The negative result for band-gap prediction highlights limitations from sample scarcity and semi-local DFT labels. The domain of applicability is uneven across anions and cations, requiring careful consideration when applying these models to new compound classes.

title: Hyperfine Structure and Exchange Coupling of Vacancy-Induced Ce$^{3+}$ Spin Centers in Nuclear-Spin-Dilute CeO$_2$
summary: Oxygen vacancies in ceria CeO$_2$ donate electrons that localize as Ce$^{3+}$ ($4f^1$, $S=1/2$) small polarons, creating rare-earth spin centers through native defect chemistry rather than implantation or extrinsic doping. We investigate the magnetic environment of these centers using first-principles PBE$+U$ calculations with a linear-response Hubbard parameter ($U=5.8382$ eV), hyperfine tensors from the all-electron reconstruction of the projector-augmented-wave method, and Korringa-Kohn-Rostoker exchange calculations within the coherent-potential approximation. Four vacancy configurations spanning concentrations from $3.125\%$ to $12.5\%$ are considered. A distinctive feature of the host follows from cerium isotopics: all naturally occurring cerium isotopes possess nuclear spin $I=0$, eliminating on-site hyperfine interactions at the Ce$^{3+}$ center and leaving the nuclear-spin bath entirely on the oxygen sublattice, whose sole magnetic isotope, $^{17}O$ ($I=5/2$), occurs at $0.038\%$ natural abundance. The resulting $^{17}O$ hyperfine landscape consists of a small number of strongly coupled, nearly axial first-shell nuclei with contact couplings reaching $6$~MHz, surrounded by a weakly coupled and strongly anisotropic outer shell. These tensors define experimentally accessible signatures for $^{17}O$ ESEEM and HYSCORE measurements and provide the microscopic hyperfine parameters required for cluster-correlation-expansion calculations of spin coherence. Exchange interactions between neighboring polarons are weak and oxygen-mediated, leaving the vacancy-generated spins largely independent over the concentration range considered. Together, these results establish oxygen-deficient CeO$_2$ as a chemically generated and intrinsically nuclear-spin-dilute host for rare-earth spin centers, and provide the first-principles magnetic parameters needed to assess their coherence properties.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates magnetic properties of oxygen vacancy-induced Ce3+ small polarons in CeO2 using first-principles calculations. The system creates rare-earth spin centers through native defect chemistry without extrinsic doping. Due to Ce isotopes having I=0, hyperfine interactions occur only at 17O nuclei (0.038% abundance), creating a nuclear-spin-dilute system with experimentally accessible signatures for advanced spectroscopic techniques.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to establish oxygen-deficient CeO2 as a chemically generated, intrinsically nuclear-spin-dilute host for rare-earth spin centers. These defects create Ce3+ (4f^1, S=1/2) small polarons through native defect chemistry rather than implantation or extrinsic doping. The researchers aim to provide microscopic hyperfine parameters needed to assess coherence properties for quantum applications.
AI.method: The study employs: (1) First-principles PBE+U calculations with linear-response Hubbard parameter (U=5.8382 eV); (2) Hyperfine tensors from all-electron reconstruction of projector-augmented-wave method; (3) Korringa-Kohn-Rostoker exchange calculations within coherent-potential approximation. Four vacancy configurations spanning concentrations from 3.125% to 12.5% were studied.
AI.result: Key results include: (1) 17O hyperfine landscape shows strongly coupled, nearly axial first-shell nuclei with contact couplings up to 6 MHz; (2) Weakly coupled, anisotropic outer shell surrounds the first shell; (3) Exchange interactions between neighboring polarons are weak and oxygen-mediated; (4) Ce isotopes (all I=0) eliminate on-site hyperfine at Ce3+ centers; (5) 17O (I=5/2) at 0.038% natural abundance is the sole magnetic isotope on oxygen sublattice.
AI.conclusion: The study establishes oxygen-deficient CeO2 as a chemically generated, intrinsically nuclear-spin-dilute host for rare-earth spin centers. The 17O hyperfine tensors provide experimentally accessible signatures for 17O ESEEM and HYSCORE measurements. These results provide the first-principles magnetic parameters required for cluster-correlation-expansion calculations of spin coherence, enabling assessment of coherence properties for quantum information applications.

title: Phase-Controlled Epitaxy and Anisotropic Antiferromagnetism of Polar Wurtzite MnTe
comment: main text pages 1-20, 6 figures; supplement pages 21-29, 5 figures
summary: Altermagnetic spintronics requires materials in which compensated magnetic order, symmetry-controlled electronic responses, and epitaxial tunability can be combined in experimentally accessible thin films. MnTe is a key material in this context, but experimental studies have focused mainly on the stable NiAs-type polymorph, whereas the polar wurtzite phase remains largely unexplored. Here we demonstrate molecular-beam epitaxy growth and investigate properties of nearly phase-pure wurtzite MnTe deposited directly on GaAs(111)B, and show that small changes in the growth conditions strongly modify the phase composition, from a multiphase state with endotaxial NiAs-type inclusions embedded in wurtzite MnTe matrix to an almost single-phase polar wurtzite layer.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates molecular-beam epitaxy growth of wurtzite MnTe on GaAs(111)B for altermagnetic spintronics applications. The researchers found that growth conditions can be tuned to produce either multiphase films with NiAs-type inclusions or nearly single-phase polar wurtzite MnTe.
AI.motivation: Altermagnetic spintronics requires materials combining compensated magnetic order, symmetry-controlled electronic responses, and epitaxial tunability in experimentally accessible thin films. MnTe is a key material for this purpose, but previous studies focused mainly on the stable NiAs-type polymorph while the polar wurtzite phase remained largely unexplored.
AI.method: The researchers used molecular-beam epitaxy (MBE) to grow wurtzite MnTe directly on GaAs(111)B substrates. They systematically investigated how small changes in growth conditions affect the phase composition of the films.
AI.result: They successfully demonstrated growth of nearly phase-pure wurtzite MnTe. The key finding was that minor adjustments to growth conditions strongly modified the phase composition - from a multiphase state with endotaxial NiAs-type inclusions embedded in a wurtzite MnTe matrix, to an almost single-phase polar wurtzite layer.
AI.conclusion: The study shows that wurtzite MnTe can be grown as thin films with controllable phase composition, making it a promising candidate for altermagnetic spintronics applications. The ability to tune between multiphase and single-phase polar wurtzite structures provides flexibility for future device development.

title: Emergent toroidal induction in a polar Weyl ferromagnet
summary: Spin-orbit coupling (SOC) underpins modern spintronics by enabling the electrical generation of spin torques. Its reciprocal counterpart, in which magnetization dynamics produce electromotive forces through a spin-dependent Berry phase, is known as emergent electromagnetic induction (EEMI). However, this effect has previously been observed only in magnetic textures with spatial gradients, such as domain walls, helices, and skyrmions. Here, we demonstrate that even a spatially uniform ferromagnet can host EEMI through a previously unrecognized Berry-phase mechanism inherent to noncentrosymmetric conductors. In the polar Weyl ferromagnet PrAlGe, an applied alternating current generates spin-orbit torques that drive collective magnetization dynamics. The resulting emergent toroidal moment (T = P \times M), where (P) is the crystal's polar axis and (M) is the net magnetization, acts as a gauge potential whose time derivative (dT/dt) induces a Hall voltage. This contribution appears specifically in the out-of-phase component of the AC Hall response and scales linearly with frequency, providing direct evidence for EEMI. First-principles calculations further reveal that this toroidal vector encodes the collective motion of Weyl nodes in momentum space. These findings establish "emergent toroidal induction" as a new manifestation of spin-orbit entanglement, unifying Berry phase, topology, and spin dynamics while opening a pathway toward intrinsic and energy-efficient spin-charge interconversion.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that emergent electromagnetic induction (EEMI) can occur in spatially uniform ferromagnets through a previously unrecognized Berry-phase mechanism in noncentrosymmetric conductors, using the polar Weyl ferromagnet PrAlGe as the experimental platform.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to demonstrate that EEMI, previously observed only in magnetic textures with spatial gradients (domain walls, helices, skyrmions), can also occur in uniform ferromagnets through a new mechanism involving emergent toroidal moments.
AI.method: The researchers used PrAlGe (a polar Weyl ferromagnet), applied alternating current to generate spin-orbit torques driving collective magnetization dynamics, and measured the resulting Hall voltage in the out-of-phase AC component that scales linearly with frequency. They also performed first-principles calculations.
AI.result: They discovered "emergent toroidal induction" where the emergent toroidal moment T = P × M (P = polar axis, M = magnetization) acts as a gauge potential whose time derivative (dT/dt) induces a Hall voltage. First-principles calculations revealed that this toroidal vector encodes collective motion of Weyl nodes in momentum space.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes "emergent toroidal induction" as a new manifestation of spin-orbit entanglement, unifying Berry phase, topology, and spin dynamics while opening pathways toward intrinsic and energy-efficient spin-charge interconversion.

title: Symmetry-Twisted Multi-Entropies: Order Parameters for 2D SPT Phases
summary: Although symmetry-protected topological phases (SPTs) can be distinguished by their entanglement properties, it has been unclear how to extract this information directly from expectation values beyond the 1D case. Here, we close this gap and propose a pair of nonlocal order parameters that can detect and distinguish all bosonic SPTs in 2D protected by internal discrete, Abelian unitary symmetries. The desired topological invariants are extracted by these quantities by effectively simulating the SPT path integral on topologically non-trivial spacetime manifolds. Our order parameters are defined in terms of expectation values of partial symmetry and permutation operations acting on fixed numbers of replicas of the system in finite spatial regions. These expectation values correspond to symmetry-twisted versions of multipartite entanglement quantities known as multi-entropies. We show explicitly that our two order parameters detect symmetry-protected four-party and six-party entanglement, respectively, and we constrain possible "spurious" contributions. We analytically test our proposal in fixed-point lattice models. Our results suggest multipartite entanglement to be a defining feature of SPTs; indeed, we expect our methods to generalize to fermionic and higher-dimensional systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method to extract topological invariants from symmetry-protected topological (SPT) phases in 2D using nonlocal order parameters defined as expectation values of partial symmetry and permutation operations. These order parameters detect symmetry-protected four-party and six-party entanglement, effectively simulating SPT path integrals on topologically non-trivial spacetime manifolds.
AI.motivation: Previous methods could distinguish SPTs by entanglement properties in 1D, but it was unclear how to extract this information from expectation values in higher dimensions (2D). This work aims to close this gap by providing concrete measurable quantities.
AI.method: The authors propose pair of nonlocal order parameters defined in terms of expectation values of partial symmetry and permutation operations acting on replicas of the system in finite spatial regions. These correspond to symmetry-twisted versions of multi-entropies (multipartite entanglement quantities). The invariants are extracted by simulating SPT path integrals on topologically non-trivial spacetime manifolds.
AI.result: The two order parameters successfully detect symmetry-protected four-party and six-party entanglement respectively. The methods were analytically tested in fixed-point lattice models, and possible "spurious" contributions were constrained.
AI.conclusion: Multipartite entanglement appears to be a defining feature of SPTs. The proposed methods can detect and distinguish all bosonic SPTs in 2D protected by internal discrete, Abelian unitary symmetries, and may generalize to fermionic and higher-dimensional systems.

title: Analytical solution of the Eliashberg equations for strong-coupling superconductivity in hydrides
comment: Accepted for publication in Annals of Physics. To appear in Annals of Physics (2026). DOI: 10.1016/j.aop.2026.170637
summary: The recent discovery of room-temperature superconductivity in hydrogen-rich materials under extreme pressures has renewed interest in phonon-mediated pairing mechanisms described by the Eliashberg theory, which generalizes the BCS framework by incorporating phonon retardation effects. In this article, we present an analytical solution to the isotropic Eliashberg equations for the superconducting critical temperature, formulated within the Debye model. This solution exhibits good agreement with fully selfconsistent numerical calculations across both weak- and strong-coupling regimes, correctly reproducing the exponential and square-root dependences on the electron-phonon coupling parameter. We further apply the solution to YH6, benchmarking against experiment and the McMillan-Allen-Dynes formula. More broadly, across a diverse set of hydride superconductors, the predicted critical temperature shows scaling consistency with ab initio calculations and experimental data.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an analytical solution to the isotropic Eliashberg equations for calculating the superconducting critical temperature (Tc) using the Debye model, which shows good agreement with numerical calculations and experimental data across various hydride superconductors including YH6.
AI.motivation: The discovery of room-temperature superconductivity in hydrogen-rich materials under high pressure has renewed interest in phonon-mediated pairing mechanisms. However, the Eliashberg theory, which generalizes BCS theory by incorporating phonon retardation effects, lacks simple analytical solutions for Tc.
AI.method: The authors developed an analytical solution to the isotropic Eliashberg equations within the Debye model framework. They benchmarked this solution against fully self-consistent numerical calculations, the McMillan-Allen-Dynes formula, and experimental data across multiple hydride superconductors.
AI.result: The analytical solution exhibits good agreement with numerical calculations in both weak- and strong-coupling regimes, correctly reproducing both exponential and square-root dependences on the electron-phonon coupling parameter. For YH6, the solution shows consistency with experiment and the McMillan-Allen-Dynes formula. Across diverse hydride superconductors, predicted Tc values show scaling consistency with ab initio calculations and experimental data.
AI.conclusion: The analytical solution provides a reliable and simpler method for predicting critical temperatures in phonon-mediated superconductors, particularly hydrogen-rich materials, and offers broader applicability across various hydride systems.

title: Magnetic Contributions to Phase Stability in the Co-Ni Binary: A First-Principles CALPHAD Study
summary: We propose a simple method to employ ab-initio calculations to determine magnetic contributions to free energy of alloys. Validation on the Co-Ni binary demonstrates that this ab initio approach reproduces experimental FCC-HCP phase equilibria while providing physically transparent, structure-dependent magnetic parameters suitable for multicomponent extrapolation. Our results demonstrate that physically grounded magnetic parametrization, derived directly from electronic structure calculations, enables predictive phase diagram modeling for magnetic alloy systems.

AI.tldr: A simple method using ab-initio calculations to determine magnetic contributions to free energy of alloys, validated on the Co-Ni binary system.
AI.motivation: The need to determine magnetic contributions to free energy for accurate phase diagram modeling of magnetic alloy systems.
AI.method: Ab-initio electronic structure calculations to derive magnetic parameters for free energy calculations.
AI.result: The approach reproduces experimental FCC-HCP phase equilibria and provides structure-dependent magnetic parameters suitable for multicomponent extrapolation.
AI.conclusion: Physically grounded magnetic parametrization derived from electronic structure calculations enables predictive phase diagram modeling for magnetic alloy systems.

title: Mapping vortices to anyons in toric code phases of generalized Kitaev models
summary: We present a comprehensive theory of mapping flux excitations, or vortices, to electric and magnetic particles in the toric code phases of generalizations of the Kitaev honeycomb model in two spatial dimensions. Our method, which is formulated with the Majorana fermion representation, utilizes the fusion rule of the Abelian anyons and the physical constraint on the fermion parity, and applies to generic model parameters including any perturbative limit. Not only are we able to reproduce the known mapping scheme in the dimer limit, we also derive the conditions for the invariance of anyon species of individual vortices. We prove that the mapping of anyons is left unchanged by any continuous evolution of model parameters that does not close the fermion gap in both the vortex-free and two-vortex sectors, which enables precise demarcations between multiple regimes associated with different maps within a single phase characterized by a trivial Chern number. We illustrate our theory via extensive computations for a number of selected models, in particular those defined on the square-octagon lattice and the honeycomb lattice with a Kekulé structure. We also demonstrate that distinct mappings of anyons can nevertheless exhibit the same weak symmetry breaking, and further argue that they belong to the same symmetry-enriched topological order.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a comprehensive theory for mapping flux excitations (vortices) to electric and magnetic particles in toric code phases of generalized Kitaev honeycomb models in 2D, using Majorana fermion representation and fusion rules with fermion parity constraints.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop a systematic method to understand the correspondence between vortices and anyon particles in topological phases, particularly across different parameter regimes of the Kitaev honeycomb model and its generalizations, which is important for understanding symmetry-enriched topological orders.
AI.method: The method employs Majorana fermion representation, utilizes fusion rules of Abelian anyons, and applies physical constraints on fermion parity. This approach is applicable to generic model parameters including any perturbative limit.
AI.result: The paper reproduces known mapping schemes in the dimer limit, derives conditions for invariance of anyon species, proves mapping invariance under continuous parameter evolution without closing the fermion gap, and demonstrates that distinct mappings can exhibit the same weak symmetry breaking while belonging to the same symmetry-enriched topological order.
AI.conclusion: The theory provides a unified framework for understanding anyon mappings across different regimes within a single topological phase, enabling precise demarcations between multiple regimes and establishing connections between different mapping schemes through symmetry properties.

title: Systematic modulation of superconducting gap dynamics in YBCO|BNT|YBCO Josephson Junctions through THz field interaction and BNT ferroelectric barrier
summary: The integration of ferroelectric barriers into high-temperature Josephson junctions offers a pathway to tunable superconducting quantum devices. Here, we demonstrate robust Josephson coupling in YBa2Cu3O7-x (YBCO)/Bi0.5Na0.5TiO3 (BNT)/YBCO trilayer junctions incorporating a 40 nm ferroelectric BNT barrier. Epitaxial trilayers were fabricated by pulsed laser deposition on SrTiO3 substrates and investigated under broadband terahertz (THz) irradiation (0.5-2.5 THz). At 1.5 THz, close to the Josephson plasma resonance, the BNT polarization increased from 33.4 to 46.4 uC/cm2 at 30 K, enhancing superconducting transport. The junctions exhibited a well-defined zero-voltage supercurrent branch, with the critical current Ic(T) remaining nearly constant up to 60 K and reaching 468.2 uA under 1.5 THz excitation, evidencing strong phase coherence. Scanning tunneling spectroscopy revealed an enhanced superconducting gap in YBCO/BNT/YBCO (Delta = 1.3 meV) compared with pure YBCO (Delta = 1.05 meV), while optical conductivity measurements showed a reduction in conductivity and gap with increasing temperature, consistent with BCS theory under THz excitation. Atomic force microscopy and X-ray reflectivity confirmed uniform morphology and sharp interfaces, excluding defect-mediated transport. Magnetic field modulation of Ic(B) exhibited a canonical Fraunhofer interference pattern, and resistance mapping revealed alternating lobes of high and low dissipation, indicating coherent Josephson tunneling. These results establish that Josephson coupling is intrinsic to the YBCO/BNT/YBCO junctions, enabled by the dipolar character and dynamic THz response of the BNT barrier. This study identifies BNT as a viable, tunable barrier material for next-generation high-Tc superconducting quantum devices.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates robust Josephson coupling in YBCO/BNT/YBCO trilayer junctions using a 40nm ferroelectric Bi0.5Na0.5TiO3 (BNT) barrier, showing enhanced superconducting transport under THz irradiation near the Josephson plasma resonance.
AI.motivation: The integration of ferroelectric barriers into high-temperature Josephson junctions offers a pathway to tunable superconducting quantum devices, which could enable next-generation quantum devices with tunable properties.
AI.method: Epitaxial YBCO/BNT/YBCO trilayers were fabricated by pulsed laser deposition on SrTiO3 substrates and investigated under broadband terahertz (THz) irradiation (0.5-2.5 THz). Multiple characterization techniques were employed: scanning tunneling spectroscopy, optical conductivity measurements, atomic force microscopy, X-ray reflectivity, and magnetic field-dependent critical current measurements.
AI.result: At 1.5 THz (near Josephson plasma resonance), BNT polarization increased from 33.4 to 46.4 μC/cm² at 30 K. The critical current Ic(T) remained nearly constant up to 60 K and reached 468.2 μA under 1.5 THz excitation. Enhanced superconducting gap (Δ = 1.3 meV vs 1.05 meV in pure YBCO) was observed. Fraunhofer interference pattern confirmed coherent Josephson tunneling.
AI.conclusion: Josephson coupling is intrinsic to YBCO/BNT/YBCO junctions, enabled by the dipolar character and dynamic THz response of the BNT barrier. BNT is identified as a viable, tunable barrier material for next-generation high-Tc superconducting quantum devices.

title: Quantized Photocurrents in Gapless Topological Matter
summary: The quantum Hall effect in gapped systems represents a defining signature of nontrivial topology. Realizing this principle in gapless matter has remained a central challenge in quantum materials. Chiral topological semimetals provide a unique platform to achieve this aim via symmetry-protected multifold crossings that act as Berry-curvature monopoles. When optical transitions are confined to a single multifold node, the resulting circular photogalvanic effect is predicted to be quantized in terms of the topological charge of the node. In real materials, however, this phenomenon remains experimentally elusive, obscured by trivial band transitions, the energy separation between the node pairs, and their relative positions with respect to the Fermi level. Here we observe a quantized circular photogalvanic effect in the chiral topological semimetal Rh0.95Ni0.05Si. Ni substitution opens a photon-energy window dominated by interband optical transitions at the Γ-point multifold node. This allows circularly polarized near- to mid-infrared pulses to drive a helicity-odd terahertz response that manifests three hallmarks of quantization: a sharp onset, a wavelength-independent plateau governed by the magnitude of monopole charge, and an abrupt cutoff imposed by Pauli blocking. Our work establishes an all-optical analogue for the quantum Hall effect and a new paradigm for topological quantization in gapless matter.

AI.tldr: This paper reports the observation of a quantized circular photogalvanic effect in the chiral topological semimetal Rh0.95Ni0.05Si, establishing an all-optical analogue for the quantum Hall effect in gapless matter.
AI.motivation: Realizing the quantum Hall effect in gapless matter has been a central challenge. While chiral topological semimetals with symmetry-protected multifold crossings (Berry-curvature monopoles) offer a promising platform, experimental observation of quantized circular photogalvanic effect has been elusive due to trivial band transitions, node pair energy separation, and Fermi level positioning.
AI.method: Ni substitution in RhSi opens a photon-energy window where interband optical transitions are confined to the Γ-point multifold node. Circularly polarized near- to mid-infrared pulses were used to drive a helicity-odd terahertz response, which was then measured to identify the hallmarks of quantization.
AI.result: The helicity-odd terahertz response in Rh0.95Ni0.05Si exhibits three key signatures of quantization: (1) a sharp onset, (2) a wavelength-independent plateau governed by the monopole charge magnitude, and (3) an abrupt cutoff imposed by Pauli blocking.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes an all-optical analogue for the quantum Hall effect and introduces a new paradigm for topological quantization in gapless matter, with potential applications in topological quantum materials and devices.

title: Logarithmic corrections to bulk and surface criticality in a three-dimensional quantum Heisenberg antiferromagnet
summary: At the bulk upper critical dimension, marginally irrelevant interactions generate multiplicative logarithmic corrections to mean-field scaling. While these corrections are well understood for bulk observables, their consequences for boundary criticality, particularly for finite-size scaling, remain much less explored. Here we combine large-scale quantum Monte Carlo simulations with boundary renormalization-group analysis to study a (3 + 1)D O(3) quantum critical point. After verifying the known logarithmically modified bulk finite-size scaling, including the correlation-length scaling governed by the logarithmic finite-size exponent \hat{\coppa}, we tune the surface coupling to identify ordinary, special, and extraordinary boundary regimes. For the ordinary and special transitions, we derive logarithmic correction exponents and \hat{\coppa}-dependent finite-size scaling forms for boundary correlations, including results that have not been systematically established before. These predictions are quantitatively supported by Monte Carlo data. In the extraordinary regime, we find long-range surface magnetic order and a logarithmically enhanced surface-bulk correlation.

AI.tldr: This paper studies logarithmic corrections to finite-size scaling at a (3+1)D O(3) quantum critical point, combining quantum Monte Carlo simulations with boundary renormalization-group analysis. It explores how marginally irrelevant interactions affect boundary criticality across ordinary, special, and extraordinary boundary regimes, deriving new scaling forms for boundary correlations.
AI.motivation: While logarithmic corrections to bulk critical phenomena at the upper critical dimension are well understood, their effects on boundary criticality and finite-size scaling remain unexplored. This work aims to fill this gap by systematically studying boundary critical behavior in a model O(3) quantum critical point.
AI.method: The authors use large-scale quantum Monte Carlo simulations combined with boundary renormalization-group analysis. They verify known logarithmically modified bulk finite-size scaling, tune surface couplings to identify different boundary regimes, and derive scaling forms for boundary correlations.
AI.result: The paper verifies bulk finite-size scaling with logarithmic corrections including the correlation-length scaling governed by the logarithmic finite-size exponent θ̂. For ordinary and special transitions, they derive logarithmic correction exponents and θ̂-dependent finite-size scaling forms for boundary correlations, with quantitative support from Monte Carlo data. In the extraordinary regime, they find long-range surface magnetic order and logarithmically enhanced surface-bulk correlation.
AI.conclusion: The study establishes a comprehensive framework for understanding logarithmic corrections to boundary criticality at (3+1)D quantum critical points, providing both theoretical predictions and numerical verification across all three boundary regimes.

title: Pokrovsky--Talapov and Berezinskii--Kosterlitz--Thouless Phase Transitions in Bilayer Superconducting Films under an In-Plane Magnetic Field
summary: We study finite-temperature phase transitions in a Josephson-coupled bilayer superconducting film with compact layer phases under an in-plane magnetic field. At zero temperature, where thermally excited layer vortices are absent, the relative-phase sector undergoes a Pokrovsky--Talapov (PT) commensurate--incommensurate (C--IC) transition from a commensurate Fulde--Ferrell (C/FF) state to an incommensurate Bloch superconducting (IC/Bloch SC) state. At finite temperature, compactness separates two distinct defect mechanisms. The C--IC boundary remains a PT soliton-entry line: interlayer Josephson vortex--antivortex-pair solitons enter with the square-root onset $ρ_{\rm sol}\propto [k_0-k_0^c(T)]^{1/2}$. Thermal melting is instead Berezinskii--Kosterlitz--Thouless (BKT)-like, with correlation exponent $η=1/4$ at the boundary, but the active vortex channel changes across the phase diagram. Josephson locking suppresses elementary layer vortices in the C/FF state and selects a same-vorticity layer-pair BKT channel, whereas elementary layer vortices control melting of the IC/Bloch SC state.

AI.tldr: This paper studies finite-temperature phase transitions in a Josephson-coupled bilayer superconducting film with compact layer phases under an in-plane magnetic field. It reveals distinct defect mechanisms at zero and finite temperatures, including a Pokrovsky-Talapov commensurate-incommensurate transition and Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless melting with different vortex channels depending on the phase state.
AI.motivation: The study aims to understand how finite-temperature phase transitions behave in Josephson-coupled bilayer superconducting films, particularly how compactness of layer phases affects defect mechanisms and the nature of phase transitions under an in-plane magnetic field.
AI.method: The researchers analyze the system theoretically, examining zero-temperature behavior where thermally excited layer vortices are absent, and finite-temperature behavior where compactness separates distinct defect mechanisms. They study the C-IC boundary and thermal melting transitions.
AI.result: At zero temperature, a Pokrovsky-Talapor (PT) transition occurs from a commensurate Fulde-Ferrell (C/FF) state to an incommensurate Bloch superconducting (IC/Bloch SC) state. At finite temperature, the C-IC boundary remains a PT soliton-entry line with square-root onset behavior. Thermal melting is BKT-like with correlation exponent η=1/4, but the active vortex channel changes: Josephson locking suppresses elementary layer vortices in C/FF state and selects same-vorticity layer-pair BKT channel, while elementary layer vortices control melting of IC/Bloch SC state.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals two distinct defect mechanisms separated by compactness: soliton-entry at the C-IC boundary and BKT-like melting, with the active vortex channel varying across the phase diagram depending on whether the system is in the C/FF or IC/Bloch SC state.

title: Many-body interactions in the dielectric theory of stopping power of solids for classical and quantum projectiles
summary: We take account of the many-body dynamic interactions by including the wave-vector and frequency-dependent exchange and correlations (xc) kernel $f_{xc}(\qv,ω)$ in the framework of the dielectric theory of the stopping power of crystals for moving charges. The cases of classical and quantum projectiles are considered. We find that (I) the role of the xc effects in slowing of charges in crystalline solids is more pronounced than it is within the jellium model and (II) For velocities below the stopping maximum, inclusion of xc leads to the improvement of the comparison of the theory with experiment over a range of solid targets. On the other hand, in the high-velocity regime, the role of the dynamic xc proves negligible, which does not contradict experiment, and which we substantiate analytically within the jellium model of the target. The input to our theory is the microscopic dielectric matrix $ε_{\Gv \Gv'}(\qv,ω)$ within the random phase approximation (i.e., with neglect of $f_{xc}$), the calculation of which matrix is implemented in the existing solid-state codes.

AI.tldr: This paper extends the dielectric theory of stopping power in crystals by incorporating the frequency and wave-vector dependent exchange-correlation kernel f_xc(q,ω), going beyond the commonly used jellium model. The study shows improved agreement with experimental data at low velocities while revealing negligible effects at high velocities.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve the theoretical description of how moving charges lose energy (stopping power) in crystalline solids by accounting for many-body dynamic interactions through the exchange-correlation kernel, which has been largely neglected in previous models.
AI.method: The method involves including the exchange-correlation kernel f_xc(q,ω) in the dielectric formalism, using the microscopic dielectric matrix ε_GG'(q,ω) within the random phase approximation (RPA) as input. Both classical and quantum projectiles are considered, and analytical results are derived within the jellium model for validation.
AI.result: The key results are: (I) Exchange-correlation effects play a more significant role in crystalline solids compared to the jellium model; (II) At velocities below the stopping maximum, including xc improves agreement with experimental data across various solid targets; (III) At high velocities, dynamic xc effects become negligible, which is consistent with experimental observations.
AI.conclusion: The conclusions are that incorporating exchange-correlation effects significantly improves the theoretical description of stopping power in crystalline solids at low velocities, while the high-velocity regime can be adequately described without dynamic xc corrections. The approach is implementable in existing solid-state codes using the RPA dielectric matrix.

title: Emergent interweaved CDW unoccupied states in hole-doping LaTe2 with element substitution
summary: Multiple CDW-ordered layered rare-earth tellurides have increasingly emerged as a research hotspot, owing to their unconventional CDW formation, high transition temperature, and confirmed existence of axial Higgs modes. Recently, interweaved CDW in LaTe2 and its element-substituted phase LaTe2-xSbx have been investigated through TEM and ARPES measurements, revealing their distinct origins. Nevertheless, several complex diffraction features observed in TEM patterns remain unelucidated. In this work, we carried out scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) on LaTe1.6Sb0.4 crystals at 9 K. Three interweaved CDW wave vectors, q1=8/11a*, q2=5/11a* and q3=3/11a* were observed, which are induced by hole doping in unoccupied states. The q1 and q3 are theoretically verified to be nesting vectors connecting px- and py- bands. Furthermore, the satellite spots relative to the main Bragg spots p corresponding to a 11-a-superlattice have also been detected. Our findings provide critical insights for further exploring the origin of the interweaved CDW in hole/electron doping materials.

AI.tldr: This study investigates interweaved charge density wave (CDW) states in LaTe1.6Sb0.4 crystals using scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) at 9 K. Three CDW wave vectors (q1=8/11a*, q2=5/11a*, q3=3/11a*) were observed, induced by hole doping in unoccupied states. The q1 and q3 vectors were theoretically verified as nesting vectors connecting px- and py- bands. A 11-a-superlattice was also detected.
AI.motivation: The research aims to understand the complex diffraction features and origins of interweaved CDW in LaTe2 and its element-substituted phases (LaTe2-xSbx), which remain unelucidated despite TEM and ARPES measurements. The study seeks to provide insights into CDW formation mechanisms in hole/electron doping materials.
AI.method: Scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) measurements were conducted on LaTe1.6Sb0.4 crystals at 9 K to directly observe CDW wave vectors and superlattice structures.
AI.result: Three interweaved CDW wave vectors (q1=8/11a*, q2=5/11a*, q3=3/11a*) were identified, all induced by hole doping in unoccupied states. The q1 and q3 vectors were theoretically confirmed as nesting vectors connecting px- and py- bands. Satellite spots corresponding to an 11-a-superlattice relative to main Bragg spots were detected.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide critical insights into understanding the origin of interweaved CDW in hole/electron doping materials, particularly demonstrating that the CDW formation in LaTe1.6Sb0.4 is driven by hole doping and band nesting between px- and py- orbitals.

title: Magnetothermopower and particle-hole symmetry in a cuprate strange metal
summary: Here, we report magnetothermopower measurements on overdoped (Bi,Pb)2(Sr,La)2CuO6+delta (Bi2201) single crystals in magnetic fields up to 35 T. Whereas the temperature dependence of the zero-field Seebeck coefficient S(T) can be captured using Boltzmann transport theory, the field-dependent response S(H) cannot. Instead, the magnetothermopower contains a large additional contribution whose field and temperature dependence is consistent with the presence of short-range superconducting order well above Tc. Combined with earlier Hall and magnetoresistance results, these data imply that the overdoped cuprate strange metal contains two transport sectors with distinct particle-hole symmetry: a conventional particle-hole-asymmetric Fermi-liquid (FL) contribution governing the Hall effect and the zero-field thermopower, and a nearly particle-hole-symmetric sector dominating the anomalous longitudinal magnetotransport. We formulate a phenomenological real-space model in which disconnected FL islands are embedded in a compensated Dirac liquid of phase-incoherent d-wave Bogoliubov quasiparticles. This picture reconciles conventional zero-field transport with anomalous magnetothermopower and magnetoresistance and offers a concrete framework for thinking about strange metallicity in overdoped cuprates.

AI.tldr: Magnetothermopower measurements on overdoped Bi2201 single crystals reveal an anomalous field-dependent Seebeck coefficient that cannot be explained by conventional Boltzmann transport theory, indicating the presence of short-range superconducting order above Tc and supporting a two-sector transport model for the cuprate strange metal.
AI.motivation: To investigate the unusual magnetotransport properties of overdoped cuprate strange metals and understand the discrepancy between conventional Fermi liquid theory predictions and experimental observations.
AI.method: Magnetothermopower measurements on overdoped (Bi,Pb)2(Sr,La)2CuO6+delta (Bi2201) single crystals in magnetic fields up to 35 T, combined with analysis of existing Hall and magnetoresistance data.
AI.result: The field-dependent Seebeck coefficient S(H) contains a large additional contribution beyond Boltzmann theory predictions, consistent with short-range superconducting order above Tc. The data reveals two distinct transport sectors: a conventional particle-hole-asymmetric Fermi liquid sector (governing Hall effect and zero-field thermopower) and a nearly particle-hole-symmetric sector dominating anomalous longitudinal magnetotransport.
AI.conclusion: A phenomenological real-space model is proposed where disconnected Fermi liquid islands are embedded in a compensated Dirac liquid of phase-incoherent d-wave Bogoliubov quasiparticles. This picture reconciles conventional zero-field transport with anomalous magnetothermopower and magnetoresistance, providing a framework for understanding strange metallicity in overdoped cuprates.

title: Compatibility of Martensitic Microstructures in Polycrystals
summary:
The paper studies martensitic microstructures in polycrystals, focussing on their compatibility across grain boundaries. After a reduction to the case of a planar grain boundary, the case when the grain boundary separates two constant gradients of zero energy is considered. It is shown that for cubic-to tetragonal transformations such a configuration can occur when the relative grain rotation is not in the cubic group. Then the case when the grain boundary separates two simple laminates of zero energy is considered, it being shown using a computer-assisted symbolic calculation that in the cubic-to-tetragonal case compatibility is only possible for a closed set of measure zero in the manifold of grain boundary normals and relative grain rotations, and that a similar slightly weaker result holds for cubic-to-orthorhombic transformations. The results suggest why higher-order laminates are often observed for such transformations.
  The Taylor set of deformation gradients is defined and studied, this set having the property that any deformation whose gradient belongs to it corresponds to a zero-energy microstructure for the polycrystal independent of grain geometry and grain rotations. New upper bounds for the Taylor set are proved for cubic-to-tetragonal and cubic-to-orthorhombic transformations, generalizing those of Bhattacharya and Kohn using the geometrically linearized theory. The bounds imply in particular a result of Peigney characterizing the positive diagonal matrices in the quasiconvex hull of three tetragonal wells, and we give a simple independent proof of this result.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates martensitic microstructures in polycrystals, focusing on compatibility across grain boundaries. For cubic-to-tetragonal transformations, compatibility across grain boundaries is only possible for a set of measure zero, explaining why higher-order laminates are commonly observed. The paper also defines and analyzes the Taylor set of deformation gradients, providing new upper bounds for cubic-to-tetragonal and cubic-to-orthorhombic transformations.
AI.motivation: Understanding martensitic phase transformations in polycrystals is crucial for materials science, as these transformations determine material properties like shape memory effects and toughness. The key challenge is understanding why certain microstructures (particularly higher-order laminates) form and how compatibility between different crystal orientations affects energy states.
AI.method: The analysis uses: (1) Reduction to planar grain boundary cases, (2) Computer-assisted symbolic calculations, (3) Geometrically linearized theory, (4) Quasiconvex analysis and envelope functions
AI.result: Key findings: (1) For cubic-to-tetragonal transformations, grain boundary compatibility requires relative grain rotation outside the cubic group; (2) True compatibility is only possible for a measure-zero set of grain boundary normals and rotations; (3) New upper bounds for the Taylor set were established, generalizing previous results by Bhattacharya and Kohn; (4) A simple independent proof of Peigney's characterization of positive diagonal matrices in the quasiconvex hull was provided.
AI.conclusion: The results explain the prevalence of higher-order laminates in cubic-to-tetragonal and cubic-to-orthorhombic transformations - they form because compatibility across single grain boundaries is extremely restrictive. The Taylor set analysis provides useful bounds for understanding zero-energy microstructures in polycrystalline materials regardless of grain geometry.

title: Giant magnetocaloric effect at low fields in triangular-lattice NdMgAl$_{11}$O$_{19}$
summary: Magnetic refrigeration in the sub-Kelvin regime requires refrigerant materials to retain a large magnetic entropy at low temperatures by suppressing magnetic ordering. Quantum spin liquids (QSLs), which evade long-range magnetic ordering while retaining strong quantum fluctuations to the lowest temperatures, therefore provide a promising platform for realizing high-performance magnetic refrigerants. Here, we investigate the magnetic ground state and the magnetocaloric effect of the hexaaluminate, NdMgAl$_{11}$O$_{19}$, in which the Nd$^{3+}$ ions form a network of triangular lattices. Magnetic susceptibility and specific heat measurements indicate a magnetically dynamic state down to 50~mK, consistent with a QSL state. Specific heat measurements further reveal substantial magnetic entropy retained below 50~mK. Quasi-adiabatic demagnetization measurements demonstrate a superior cooling performance of NdMgAl$_{11}$O$_{19}$, which can be cooled to 113~mK from 1.9~K by only a small magnetic field change of 2~T. The outstanding refrigeration performance is attributed to the persistent spin fluctuations associated with the QSL-like ground state, together with a large effective \textit{g} factor and the smallness of the exchange interactions along the easy-axis direction. This study demonstrates that frustration, combined with strong spin-orbit coupling and crystal-electric-field effect in the rare earth magnets provides a promising design principle for next-generation cryogenic magnetic refrigerants.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that NdMgAl11O19, a frustrated triangular lattice material, exhibits quantum spin liquid behavior down to 50 mK and achieves magnetic refrigeration to 113 mK from 1.9 K using only a 2 T magnetic field change, establishing rare-earth hexaaluminates as promising candidates for next-generation cryogenic magnetic refrigerants.
AI.motivation: Magnetic refrigeration in the sub-Kelvin regime requires refrigerant materials with large magnetic entropy at low temperatures. Quantum spin liquids (QSLs), which avoid magnetic ordering while retaining strong quantum fluctuations, are identified as ideal platforms for high-performance magnetic refrigerants.
AI.method: Magnetic susceptibility and specific heat measurements were performed to investigate the magnetic ground state. Quasi-adiabatic demagnetization measurements were conducted to evaluate the cooling performance of NdMgAl11O19.
AI.result: NdMgAl11O19 exhibits a magnetically dynamic state down to 50 mK, consistent with QSL behavior. Substantial magnetic entropy is retained below 50 mK. The material achieves cooling to 113 mK from 1.9 K with only a 2 T magnetic field change, demonstrating superior cooling performance.
AI.conclusion: The outstanding refrigeration performance arises from persistent spin fluctuations in the QSL-like ground state, combined with a large effective g factor and small exchange interactions. This study establishes that frustration, strong spin-orbit coupling, and crystal-electric-field effects in rare-earth magnets provide a promising design principle for next-generation cryogenic magnetic refrigerants.

title: Fractionalized Fermi liquids with the ghost-Gutzwiller Ansatz
comment: 5 pages of main text with 2 figures, and 7 pages of supplemental material
summary: Fractionalized Fermi liquids ($\mathrm{FL}^{\!*}$), elusive metallic states characterized by fractionalized quasiparticles alongside conventional ones and defying Luttinger's theorem, are prime candidates for the pseudogap regime of underdoped cuprates. We show that a $\mathrm{FL}^{\!*}$ phase emerges in the single-band $t$-$J$ model through a simple ghost-Gutzwiller Ansatz, optimized at the cost of a mean-field calculation. The resulting temperature-doping phase diagram encompasses a low-doping $\mathrm{FL}^{\!*}$, a $d$-wave superconducting dome, and an overdoped conventional Fermi liquid, thereby reproducing key qualitative features of cuprate phenomenology.

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates that fractionalized Fermi liquids (FL*) emerge naturally in the t-J model using a ghost-Gutzwiller Ansatz, providing a theoretical framework for understanding the pseudogap phase in underdoped cuprates.
AI.motivation: Fractionalized Fermi liquids (FL*) are candidate states for the pseudogap regime in underdoped cuprates, characterized by fractionalized quasiparticles and violation of Luttinger's theorem, but their emergence from microscopic models has been challenging.
AI.method: The authors use a ghost-Gutzwiller Ansatz optimized at the cost of a mean-field calculation applied to the single-band t-J model.
AI.result: The resulting temperature-doping phase diagram exhibits three key phases: a low-doping FL* phase, a d-wave superconducting dome, and an overdoped conventional Fermi liquid, matching cuprate phenomenology.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a minimal theoretical framework that reproduces key qualitative features of cuprate phase diagram, supporting FL* as the pseudogap state.

title: Emergent exchange bias in ultra-thin La0.67Sr0.33MnO3 films driven by ferro-antiferromagnetic phase coexistence
summary: Ultra-thin La0.67Sr0.33MnO3 (LSMO) films are generally regarded as single-phase ferromagnets, yet their reduced dimensionality enhances the impact of oxygen stoichiometry and local structural distortions. Here we demonstrate that LSMO layers with thicknesses between 3 and 17 nm develop a robust and thickness-independent exchange bias (EB) despite the absence of an engineered ferromagnetic-antiferromagnetic (FM-AFM) interface. Angular magneto-optical Kerr effect measurements reveal a reproducible hysteresis-loop shift that reverses sign upon 180-degree rotation, confirming its intrinsic origin. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy shows a thickness-independent Mn2+ fraction (about 20 percent), indicating the presence of oxygen-deficient reduced regions embedded within the Mn3+/Mn4+ ferromagnetic matrix. A simple interfacial-exchange model suggests that only a sub-percent fraction of antiferromagnetic (or frustrated spin-ice-like) regions is required to generate the observed EB. These results demonstrate that ultra-thin LSMO can spontaneously host internal FM-AFM interfaces driven by oxygen deficiency, revealing an emergent route to exchange bias in nominally single-phase manganite films.

AI.tldr: Ultra-thin LSMO films (3-17 nm) exhibit robust thickness-independent exchange bias (EB) without engineered FM-AFM interfaces. Oxygen deficiency creates internal interfaces between ferromagnetic matrix and reduced regions, generating EB through interfacial exchange interactions.
AI.motivation: Ultra-thin LSMO films are typically considered single-phase ferromagnets, but their reduced dimensionality enhances sensitivity to oxygen stoichiometry and local structural distortions, potentially creating spontaneous internal interfaces.
AI.method: Angular magneto-optical Kerr effect measurements to detect hysteresis-loop shift; X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy to analyze Mn valence states; simple interfacial-exchange model for quantitative analysis.
AI.result: Thickness-independent EB with 180-degree sign reversal was observed. XPS revealed ~20% Mn2+ fraction (thickness-independent), indicating oxygen-deficient reduced regions embedded in the ferromagnetic matrix. Modeling suggests only sub-percent fraction of antiferromagnetic or frustrated spin-ice-like regions is needed to generate the observed EB.
AI.conclusion: Ultra-thin LSMO can spontaneously host internal FM-AFM interfaces driven by oxygen deficiency, revealing an emergent route to exchange bias in nominally single-phase manganite films without artificial engineering.

title: Zero-Field Long Range Order at $T \sim 40$ mK in the Proximate Quantum Spin Ice Ce$_2$Sn$_2$O$_7$ and Phase Diagram for Magnetic Fields Along [1,1,0]
comment: Main Text (5 pages, 4 figures), Supplemental Material (6 pages, 4 figures)
summary: The Ce$^{3+}$ pseudospin-1/2 degrees of freedom in the pyrochlore magnets Ce$_2$X$_2$O$_7$, with $X$ = Zr, Hf, or Sn, possess dipole-octupole character. The XYZ nearest-neighbor model Hamiltonians which have successfully described their properties make them attractive candidates for quantum spin ice ground states. We report new heat capacity measurements to very low temperatures on a high-quality single crystal of Ce$_2$Sn$_2$O$_7$ grown by hydrothermal techniques. Our zero-field measurements uncover a clear first order transition to long-ranged order at $T \sim 0.04$ K, well below the downturn in the broader Schottky-like anomaly that is a common feature in the zero-field heat capacity for cerium pyrochlores. This observation settles the debate as to whether Ce$_2$Sn$_2$O$_7$ possesses a QSI ground state in zero field - it does not. However, we also find compelling evidence suggesting that spin ice physics is nearby, and remains relevant to Ce$_2$Sn$_2$O$_7$. Application of a magnetic field along the $[1,1,0]$ direction leads to an evolution of this peak to a weak anomaly at higher temperature more characteristic of a continuous, mean field transition. At higher fields along $[1,1,0]$, the Schottky-like anomaly bifurcates similar to expectations for independent polarized $α$ and orthogonal $β$ chains in classical spin ice. These new experimental results demonstrate richness to the phase diagram for Ce-based pyrochlores.

AI.tldr: This paper reports heat capacity measurements on Ce2Sn2O7, revealing a first-order transition to long-ranged order at ~0.04 K, settling the debate that this material does not possess a quantum spin ice (QSI) ground state in zero field. However, spin ice physics remains relevant, as evidenced by field-dependent measurements showing behavior similar to classical spin ice chains.
AI.motivation: The motivation was to resolve the debate about whether Ce2Sn2O7 possesses a QSI ground state in zero field, and to explore the phase diagram of Ce-based pyrochlore magnets more broadly.
AI.method: The researchers performed heat capacity measurements on a high-quality Ce2Sn2O7 single crystal (grown by hydrothermal techniques) at very low temperatures, with and without applied magnetic fields along the [1,1,0] direction.
AI.result: A clear first-order transition to long-ranged order was found at T ~ 0.04 K, below a Schottky-like anomaly. In applied field along [1,1,0], the peak evolves into a weak anomaly at higher temperature (continuous, mean field-like), and at higher fields the Schottky anomaly bifurcates similar to expectations for independent polarized α and orthogonal β chains in classical spin ice.
AI.conclusion: Ce2Sn2O7 does not have a QSI ground state in zero field, but spin ice physics remains relevant. The phase diagram for Ce-based pyrochlores shows rich behavior, with the study demonstrating the importance of detailed experimental work to understand these complex magnetic systems.

title: Emergent $s+id$ Superconductivity from the Interplay between Electronic Correlations and Electron-Phonon Coupling in $\mathrm{R}_{1-x}\mathrm{Sr}_x\mathrm{NiO}_2$
summary: Recent tunneling measurements on infinite-layer nickelates have revealed spatially varying superconducting symmetries, whose microscopic origin remains unclear. Motivated by this observation, we investigate the interplay between electron correlations and electron-phonon interactions in infinite-layer nickelates by combining first-principles calculations with the fluctuation-exchange-Migdal-Eliashberg theory. Our calculations show that spin fluctuations yield robust $d$-wave superconductivity on the Ni $d_{x^2-y^2}$ orbital, whereas electron-phonon coupling induces $s$-wave pairing on an interstitial orbital, leading to an $s+id$ superconducting state. The emergence of the $s$-wave component is strongly carrier-density dependent: an intermediate electron-phonon coupling of $λ=0.4$ stabilizes the $s+id$ state at $n=0.9$ but not at $n=0.8$. These results imply that local oxygen defects tune the local electron density and form finite-size domains with distinct pairing symmetries, offering a compelling explanation for the spatially inhomogeneous superconducting symmetries observed in experiments.

AI.tldr: This study investigates the origin of spatially varying superconducting symmetries in infinite-layer nickelates, finding that spin fluctuations produce d-wave pairing while electron-phonon coupling induces s-wave pairing, leading to an s+id state whose stability depends on carrier density.
AI.motivation: Recent tunneling measurements revealed spatially varying superconducting symmetries in infinite-layer nickelates, but the microscopic origin was unclear. This motivated investigation into the interplay between electron correlations and electron-phonon interactions.
AI.method: The study combined first-principles calculations with fluctuation-exchange-Migdal-Eliashberg theory to investigate the interplay between electron correlations and electron-phonon interactions in infinite-layer nickelates.
AI.result: Spin fluctuations yield robust d-wave superconductivity on the Ni d_{x^2-y^2} orbital, while electron-phonon coupling induces s-wave pairing on an interstitial orbital, resulting in an s+id superconducting state. The s-wave component is strongly carrier-density dependent: λ=0.4 stabilizes the s+id state at n=0.9 but not at n=0.8.
AI.conclusion: Local oxygen defects tune local electron density and form finite-size domains with distinct pairing symmetries, providing a compelling explanation for the spatially inhomogeneous superconducting symmetries observed in experiments.

title: Antiphase boundary-driven large exchange bias and negative magnetoresistance in NiCo2O4
summary: Nickel-based spinel oxide has recently attracted significant attention due to its remarkable magneto-transport properties, promising various spintronic applications. In this study, we observe an exchange bias effect, where the magnetic hysteresis loop shifts both horizontally and vertically when single-phase nanocrystalline NiCo2O4 is cooled in the presence of an external magnetic field. The magnitude of the exchange bias field is approximately 375 Oe at 5 K, and this effect disappears around 300 K. Furthermore, NiCo2O4 shows semiconducting behaviour in longitudinal resistivity and demonstrates a substantial negative magnetoresistance of ~31.5% at 10 K. The magnetoresistance exhibits a butterfly-shaped behaviour at low magnetic fields and becomes almost linear at high magnetic fields. A detailed analysis of the negative magnetoresistance and exchange bias reveals that both phenomena originate from the formation of antiphase boundaries in the as-synthesized NiCo2O4 sample, as confirmed by the high-resolution transmission electron micrograph.

AI.tldr: This study reports exchange bias effect and negative magnetoresistance in nanocrystalline NiCo2O4 spinel oxide, with exchange bias field of ~375 Oe at 5K disappearing at 300K, and magnetoresistance of ~31.5% at 10K, both attributed to antiphase boundaries.
AI.motivation: To investigate magneto-transport properties of nickel-based spinel oxide for spintronic applications, specifically to understand the origin of exchange bias and magnetoresistance effects.
AI.method: Single-phase nanocrystalline NiCo2O4 was synthesized and studied through magnetic hysteresis measurements at various temperatures, magnetoresistance measurements, and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM).
AI.result: Exchange bias field of ~375 Oe observed at 5K (disappears at 300K); negative magnetoresistance of ~31.5% at 10K with butterfly-shaped behavior at low fields and linear behavior at high fields; antiphase boundaries confirmed by HRTEM.
AI.conclusion: Both exchange bias and negative magnetoresistance originate from antiphase boundaries in the as-synthesized NiCo2O4 sample, making this material promising for spintronic applications.

title: Measurement-induced phase transition in space
summary: Measurement-induced phase transitions (MIPTs) in monitored quantum circuits are usually characterized by preparing steady states at different uniform measurement probabilities. Here we introduce a spatial realization of the MIPT by imposing a deterministic measurement gradient in a single monitored Clifford chain. The resulting steady state contains coexisting volume-law, critical, and area-law regions, with the point $p(x)=p_c$ acting as a spatial critical cut. By scanning entanglement observables across this profile, we show that the transition is organized by a spatial scaling form. Although this structure is analogous to finite-time scaling in temporally driven MIPT, the spatial protocol has no Kibble-Zurek dynamics. Instead, the physical bounds $0\le p\le 1$ impose a finite linear window, producing cutoff-controlled asymptotic regimes whose fitted exponents provide direct access to the correlation-length exponent $ν$. Our results establish spatially inhomogeneous measurements as a controlled route to engineer and probe measurement-induced criticality within a single steady state.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a spatial realization of measurement-induced phase transitions (MIPTs) by implementing a deterministic measurement gradient in a single monitored Clifford chain, producing a steady state with coexisting volume-law, critical, and area-law regions that allows direct measurement of the correlation-length exponent ν.
AI.motivation: Traditional MIPT studies characterize transitions at different uniform measurement probabilities. This work aims to create a spatial version where measurement probability varies across space in a single system, enabling novel probes of measurement-induced criticality within one steady state.
AI.method: The authors impose a spatial measurement gradient (deterministic measurement probability p(x) varying with position x) in a monitored Clifford chain. They scan entanglement observables across the resulting spatial profile to identify the transition point p(x)=p_c acting as a spatial critical cut.
AI.result: The steady state contains coexisting volume-law, critical, and area-law regions. The transition is organized by a spatial scaling form. Unlike temporal MIPT with Kibble-Zurek dynamics, the spatial protocol has none. The finite linear window from physical bounds 0≤p≤1 produces cutoff-controlled asymptotic regimes.
AI.conclusion: Spatially inhomogeneous measurements provide a controlled route to engineer and probe measurement-induced criticality within a single steady state. The fitted exponents from cutoff-controlled regimes give direct access to correlation-length exponent ν.

title: The self-organized vacancy order in Pr$_9$Ge$_{16}$
comment: accepted for publication in Phys. Rev. Mater
summary: In this work, we report the discovery of a new crystal structure on the Ge-rich side of the Pr-Ge binary phase diagram. Using a high-temperature flux technique, we grew single crystals of $Pr_9Ge_{16}$, which adopt a previously unreported orthorhombic $Fdd$2 structure type featuring ordered Ge vacancies. We present the anisotropic magnetic properties and identify the crystallographic $b$ axis perpendicular to the crystal plane as the magnetic easy axis. Temperature-dependent resistivity measurements reveal metallic behavior with a distinct anomaly at $T_{\mathrm{C}}$ = 14.3 K. Hall resistivity data indicate that electron-like carriers dominate, with a carrier concentration on the order of $10^{27}~\mathrm{m}^{-3}$. The magnetic order is readily suppressed by a magnetic field of approximately 0.4 T applied along the easy $b$ axis.
